frontier_justification: This direction remains a May 2026 frontier because formal theorem proving and RL-based math reasoning have advanced quickly, yet most progress still depends on formalizable targets or automatically verifiable answers. Research mathematics requires conjecture formation, strategy discovery, and progress estimation before a clean reward signal exists.

papers.title: Measuring Mathematical Problem Solving With the MATH Dataset
papers.key_contribution: Introduces the MATH dataset of 12,500 competition math problems with step-by-step solutions, establishing the canonical benchmark for measuring mathematical reasoning in AI.

idea_genome.problem_genome: No rigorous benchmark exists for measuring AI mathematical reasoning at competition level; existing benchmarks (GSM8K) test only basic arithmetic and word problems.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Curate 12,500 problems from AMC, AIME, and other competitions across 7 subjects with full step-by-step solutions; provide an auxiliary pretraining dataset (AMPS) of 100K+ problems to teach fundamentals.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Large language models achieve only 6.9% accuracy on MATH at the time of release; even with AMPS pretraining, GPT-2 reaches only 8.8%; the benchmark exposes a vast gap in mathematical reasoning.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Only covers competition-style problems with unique numerical answers; no proof-based problems; static benchmark that will eventually saturate as models improve.

papers.title: Solving Quantitative Reasoning Problems with Language Models
papers.key_contribution: Minerva achieves 50.3% on MATH by continued pretraining of PaLM on technical content, demonstrating that domain-specific pretraining on scientific and mathematical text dramatically improves quantitative reasoning.

idea_genome.problem_genome: General-purpose LLMs fail on quantitative reasoning despite strong language abilities; MATH benchmark accuracy is below 10% for most models.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Continue pretraining PaLM (540B) on a curated dataset of scientific papers, math web pages, and LaTeX documents; use majority voting over sampled solutions at inference time; no tool use or external verification.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Minerva 540B reaches 50.3% on MATH (7x improvement over prior SOTA); 78.5% on GSM8K; correctly answers nearly a third of undergraduate STEM exam problems; performance scales with model size and data.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Closed-source model and dataset; majority voting is compute-expensive; no step-level verification; struggles with multi-step proofs and problems requiring novel mathematical insight.

papers.title: Let's Verify Step by Step
papers.key_contribution: Process Reward Models (PRMs) trained with step-level human feedback substantially outperform outcome-level reward models for mathematical reasoning verification, solving 78% of MATH problems.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Outcome-based reward models cannot identify which step in a reasoning chain is wrong; Minerva-style majority voting treats all reasoning paths equally regardless of intermediate correctness.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Collect 800K step-level human annotations of reasoning correctness (PRM800K); train a Process Reward Model that scores each intermediate step; use PRM to rerank candidate solutions from a generator.
idea_genome.observation_genome: PRM-guided best-of-N selection achieves 78.2% on MATH, substantially outperforming outcome reward models; process supervision provides more reliable signal for identifying correct reasoning.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Step-level annotation is expensive and hard to scale; PRM is a separate verifier model, not integrated into generation; requires a strong base generator to produce candidates worth verifying.

papers.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
papers.key_contribution: Introduces GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) for math reasoning and achieves 51.7% on MATH with a 7B open model, approaching GPT-4 level through careful data curation and RL.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Open-source math LLMs lag far behind closed models; PPO-based RL for reasoning is memory-intensive and unstable; high-quality math pretraining data at scale is hard to obtain.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Meticulous data selection pipeline extracts 120B math tokens from Common Crawl; continued pretraining of DeepSeek-Coder-Base; GRPO replaces PPO by normalizing rewards within a group of sampled outputs, eliminating the critic model.
idea_genome.observation_genome: DeepSeekMath 7B achieves 51.7% on MATH without tools, approaching Minerva 540B; GRPO is more memory-efficient than PPO; data quality matters more than quantity for math pretraining.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: GRPO uses only outcome-level correctness signals (no process rewards); 7B model still far below frontier closed models; reasoning chains lack self-verification or backtracking capabilities.

papers.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
papers.key_contribution: Pure RL (GRPO) without supervised CoT distillation produces emergent long chain-of-thought reasoning with self-verification, achieving 79.8% on AIME 2024 and 97.3% on MATH-500.

idea_genome.problem_genome: SFT-distilled reasoning models plateau; process reward models require costly annotation; need a scalable approach that lets reasoning capabilities emerge from RL alone without human-crafted reasoning templates.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Apply GRPO with rule-based verifiable rewards (correctness checking) directly to a base model without any supervised chain-of-thought data; the model discovers long reasoning chains, self-reflection, and verification behaviors purely through RL.
idea_genome.observation_genome: DeepSeek-R1 achieves 79.8% on AIME 2024 matching OpenAI o1; 97.3% on MATH-500; emergent behaviors include self-verification, strategy switching, and extended deliberation; open-weights release enables community research.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Requires verifiable reward signals limiting applicability to math/code; language mixing artifacts in reasoning chains; RL training is compute-intensive and unstable; no formal verification of reasoning steps.

papers.title: DeepSeek-Prover-V2: Advancing Formal Mathematical Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Subgoal Decomposition
papers.key_contribution: Combines RL-trained subgoal decomposition with formal verification to achieve 88.9% on miniF2F-test and 49/658 on PutnamBench, bridging informal mathematical reasoning with formal proof generation.

idea_genome.problem_genome: DeepSeek-R1 produces impressive informal reasoning but cannot formally verify its steps; formal provers lack the high-level problem decomposition ability of reasoning LLMs; the informal-formal gap limits both approaches.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Train an RL-based subgoal decomposition module that breaks complex problems into verifiable substeps; each subgoal is independently proved in Lean 4; combine informal chain-of-thought planning (from R1 lineage) with formal tactic generation.
idea_genome.observation_genome: 88.9% pass ratio on miniF2F-test (SOTA); 49/658 problems solved on PutnamBench; subgoal decomposition enables solving problems too complex for single-pass proving; RL training produces effective decomposition strategies.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Subgoal decomposition can fail on problems requiring non-hierarchical proof strategies; Lean 4 expressiveness limits problem scope; still requires significant compute for search over decompositions.

edges.evolution_focus: The MATH benchmark reveals the vast gap in AI mathematical reasoning, creating an ecological niche that Minerva fills through domain-specific pretraining. Minerva radiates into this newly defined problem space by applying the scaling paradigm (larger models + more domain data) to the quantitative reasoning challenge that MATH benchmarked, achieving a 7x improvement.

Process reward models represent a fundamental speciation from the generation-centric approach of Minerva. Instead of improving the generator, PRMs create an entirely new verification paradigm: training a separate model to judge each reasoning step. This shifts the bottleneck from generation quality to verification quality, establishing a distinct branch of research focused on reward modeling for reasoning.

DeepSeekMath hybridizes Minerva's domain-specific pretraining approach with ideas from the PRM verification paradigm. GRPO internalizes the reward signal that PRMs provide externally, using group-relative outcome rewards to directly optimize the generator without a separate verifier. This merges the data-scaling and reward-modeling lineages into a unified RL training recipe.

DeepSeek-R1 applies GRPO (inherited from DeepSeekMath) at much larger scale with a critical simplification: removing all supervised chain-of-thought data and letting reasoning emerge purely from RL. This is a mutation within the same GRPO framework that discovers emergent reasoning behaviors (self-verification, backtracking) not present in the parent model's design.

DeepSeek-Prover-V2 hybridizes the informal reasoning capabilities of the R1 lineage (emergent CoT, self-verification) with formal theorem proving in Lean 4. The RL-trained subgoal decomposition bridges the gap between informal mathematical intuition and formal proof, combining two previously separate paradigms into a system that reasons informally and proves formally.

open_question: DeepSeek-Prover-V2 narrows the gap between informal reasoning and Lean-verified theorem proving, but its training and search still rely on formalizable statements and verifiable subgoals. What training and evaluation mechanism would extend RL-based mathematical reasoning to open-ended research mathematics, where the system must propose conjectures, choose proof strategies, and estimate progress before a formal verification target or automatic reward exists?

question.name: Question
question.description: Present only the domain/subfield and the curated May-2026 research question; ask the model to propose a next-step research direction in Mathematical Reasoning / Math LLMs.

library.name: Library
library.description: Present the curated research frontier plus 6 trace papers in random order with paper titles and brief abstracts, but without explicit lineage order, genome fields, or dynamics labels; ask the model to propose the next research direction.

lineage.name: Lineage
lineage.description: Present the curated research frontier plus the ordered trace with genome metadata, GenomeDiff-style change evidence, and parent-child relationships; ask the model to predict the next evolutionary step without naming predefined category labels.

frontier_evidence.title: DeepSeek-Prover-V2: Advancing Formal Mathematical Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Subgoal Decomposition
frontier_evidence.unresolved_gap: Subgoal decomposition can fail on problems requiring non-hierarchical proof strategies; Lean 4 expressiveness limits problem scope; still requires significant compute for search over decompositions.
frontier_evidence.why_frontier: Used as the May 2026 frontier anchor: the arena question targets a limitation that remains unresolved at the endpoint of this lineage.

frontier_status.manual_review: Re-audited on 2026-05-05 for appendix release: active arena trace has a 2025/2026 frontier endpoint, a non-solved open research question, valid forward lineage edges, and generation settings aligned with post-hoc dynamics evaluation.
frontier_status.prompt_role: The same research question is shown in Question, Library, and Lineage settings; only the supporting literature/lineage context changes.
